figures.caption: Fig. 3: Target-Bench results. Wan-R1 achieves the highest overall score, with lower displacement errors (ADE, FDE) and miss rate, and higher soft endpoint (SE) and approach consistency (AC) compared to both the base model and Wan-SFT.

Fig. 6: Trajectory tracking across five maze types (This figure is from [45]). Green: ground-truth trajectory; Blue: tracked trajectory from generated video. Columns show temporally ordered frames illustrating agent motion over time.
Fig. 1: VLLM fails to provide reliable reward, verified rewards can guarantee correct supervision.

Fig. 7: Overview of VR-Bench (This figure is from VR-Bench [45]) (A) Maze Types: Five maze types—Regular, Irregular, 3D, Trapfield, and Sokoban—spanning 2D/3D settings and diverse task structures. (B) Reasoning Paradigm: Chain-of-frame reasoning [40] requiring frame-by-frame inferences for sequential visual reasoning. (C) Benchmark Performance: Leading VLMs evaluated on four metrics across all maze types. (D) Additional Analysis: Evaluations on difficulty, texture, and maze-type generalization, plus test-time scaling.
Table 2: Difficulty generalization performance of Wan-R1 on VR-Bench. Each task block

Fig. 8: Overview of Target-Bench (This figure is from Target-Bench [37]). Target-Bench introduces a quadruped-robot dataset and benchmark for assessing world models in mapless navigation toward text-specified goals with implicit semantic content. Given a single camera frame and a natural language description of the desired target state, world models must synthesize a future video depicting the trajectory to that goal. A world decoder then recovers the planned path from the generated video, which is evaluated against ground-truth maneuvers collected from a human-operated quadruped.

Fig. 4: Test-time scaling performance of Wan-R1 on Irregular Maze tasks. Performance is evaluated across varying numbers of samples (K ∈ {1, 4, 8, 12, 16}) and three difficulty levels (Easy, Medium, Hard). RL improves base capability (K=1) while maintaining beneficial scaling with increased sampling.
Table 6: Texture generalization performance across different visual skins. For each task,

Table 1: VR-Bench comprises five tasks: Base (Regular Maze), Irreg (Irregular Maze), Trap (TrapField), 3D (3D Maze), and Soko (Sokoban). Last row shows the performance gain compared with Wan2.2-TI2V base model.

Fig. 5: Given the video output along with the evaluation prompt describing maze-solving rules, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct produces a step-by-step analysis and assigns a reward score. Although the generated video exhibits visible glitches and degraded visual quality, the model assigns a perfect score of 1.0. This illustrates the core vulnerability of VLMbased reward models: rather than detecting actual visual artifacts, the model hallucinates quality based on high-level semantic cues—a visible agent, a recognizable maze structure, and apparent goal-directed motion—and incorrectly concludes that the video contains “no glitches, noise, or artifacts.”

Table 5: Performance comparison between the baseline model (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B), taskspecific Wan-SFT models, and our method across four game types: Base, Irreg, 3D, and Soko. Each cell displays the absolute performance alongside the relative change from the baseline across four metrics (EM, SR, PR, SD).
